questions: At CyanGate, you led the development of the **Atlassian Jira Intelligence System**, which involved coordinating between technical implementation (Python pipelines, Copilot Studio) and non-technical stakeholders who likely had strong opinions about how ticket analytics should work. **Tell me about a specific moment where a key stakeholder—perhaps a Jira power user or a department head—pushed back aggressively against your proposed solution.** How did you navigate their resistance without damaging the relationship? Walk me through the *exact words* you used to align them, and what compromise (if any) you had to make to keep the project moving forward.

Your **JSON-to-JSON Agentic System** project at CyanGate required you to achieve 95% accuracy with a *single LLM*—a significant cost and latency reduction—but the validation framework you built was entirely new to your team. **Describe a time when a junior engineer or a peer on this project failed to adopt your validation approach, leading to errors in production.** How did you diagnose whether this was a *skills gap*, a *communication failure*, or *resistance to change*? What non-technical steps did you take to ensure buy-in, and how did you measure whether your intervention actually improved team performance?

You’ve worked in a high-stakes, fast-moving environment at CyanGate where you were both an **AI Engineer** *and* a **Technical Consultant**—juggling client expectations, internal deadlines, and unproven agentic architectures. **Tell me about a project where you had to deliver bad news to a client—perhaps a missed deadline, a scaled-back feature, or an unexpected cost—because of an AI system’s unpredictability.** How did you frame the conversation to maintain trust? Did you involve your manager, or handle it independently? What did you learn about *when to escalate* versus *when to own the problem* yourself?
